text: I had my hair cut yesterday.
I got the internet set up.
I need to get my documents translated.
Hi, I've just settled into the area and I'd like to get my air conditioner fixed. Can I book an appointment?
Of course. We can have a technician come on Thursday. Would you like a quote before we start the work?
Yes, please. And while you're here, could I also have the old filter replaced?
No problem. There's a little paperwork to sign, and we ask for a small deposit to confirm.
That's fine. I'd rather get it all sorted now than deal with the red tape later.
Perfect. You'll have everything up and running by Thursday evening. Welcome to the neighbourhood!
I'd like to get my hair cut.
Could I get a quote first?
I need to get the internet set up.
I'll get it all sorted today.

When you first move somewhere new, the to-do list is endless. My first week abroad, I felt like I spent the whole time getting things sorted. The apartment was lovely, but nothing was quite ready. On the first morning I had the locks changed, because the landlord recommended it, and I got the internet set up so I could finally video-call my family. There was a bit of red tape, of course. I had to have a few documents translated and certified, and the paperwork took longer than I expected. By the middle of the week I was getting the hang of it. I booked an appointment at a little salon around the corner and had my hair cut, which made me feel human again. I took my old winter coat to the dry cleaner's, dropped it off, and had it cleaned. I even got the car checked before a long drive, and the mechanic gave me a fair quote. The strange thing is, none of this is glamorous, but it's exactly what makes a place feel like home. You don't really live somewhere until you've had things fixed, set up, and sorted in your own name. After twenty lessons, I can honestly arrange all of it in English now, and that feels like the whole journey coming together.

Hi Pricila, it's me, just leaving you a quick voice note now that you've moved in. You asked me where to get things done around the neighbourhood, so here are my honest recommendations. For your hair, there's a lovely salon on the main street. Book an appointment a few days ahead, because the good stylist gets busy. If you need documents translated for the paperwork, don't use the big agency near the station. They charge a fortune. There's a small office two streets away that does it properly and gives you a written quote first. For the car, take it to Tony's garage. He'll get it fixed honestly and won't invent problems. And if you want the apartment painted before winter, my neighbour did his last year. He had the whole place done in two days and it cost less than you'd think. Oh, and one tip. When you sign up for the internet, ask them to set everything up for you so you don't have to wrestle with it yourself. There's always a bit of red tape, but once it's all sorted, you'll feel completely settled in. Welcome to the neighbourhood. Call me if you need anything.

First, Pricila settled into her new apartment, but there was a lot to get sorted. Then she had the locks changed and got the internet set up, after a little red tape with the paperwork. Next, she booked an appointment at a salon and finally had her hair cut the way she likes it. After that, she dropped her winter coat off at the dry cleaner's and had it cleaned. So now, with everything sorted, she feels completely at home abroad.
